sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=17, col=20), MOUSE(row=34, col=13).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 111, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 103 actions; a strong score needs about 38 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Now I have clear evidence:
- (17,20): changes ALL rows [17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 41, 44] - rotates ALL rings
- (34,13): changes only row [35] - rotates only bottom ring (partially)
- (25,13): changes only row [26] - rotates only middle ring
So the triggers are:
- (17,20): rotates all rings
- (25,13): rotates middle ring only
- (34,13): rotates bottom ring only (row 35)
This is very different from what I thought earlier! (34,13) only rotates row 35, not all rings. And (17,20) rotates all rings.
Now I understand the structure:
- (17,20) is the main trigger that rotates all rings
- (25,13) is a secondary trigger that rotates only the middle ring
- (34,13) is a tertiary trigger that rotates only the bottom ring (row 35)
The completion condition might be:
1. All rings need to be in a specific configuration
2. The strip needs to reach a specific value
Since I've been rotating with different triggers, the rings might be out of sync. Let me try rotating with (17,20) until the level completes.
But wait, I've already rotated with (17,20) multiple times (1 at the start, 1 just now). The level hasn't completed. So maybe the completion condition is more complex.
Let me think about this differently. The level has 3 independent rings, and each ring can be rotated independently using different triggers. The target configuration might be one where all 3 rings are in a specific configuration.
Since (17,20) rotates all rings, and (25,13) and (34,13) rotate specific rings, I can use these triggers to align the rings independently.
But I don't know what the target configuration is. Let me try to figure it out by looking at the current image.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the level might have a completion condition that involves the blocks forming a specific color pattern. For example, the target might have all blocks of the same color adjacent to the yellow markers.
Let me look at the current image more carefully. I see:
- Top yellow marker at row 17, col 29
- Middle yellow marker at row 26, col 29
- Bottom yellow marker at row 41, col 23 (wait, looking at the image, the bottom yellow marker seems to be at a different position)
Actually, looking at the current image, I see the yellow markers are at:
- Top: row 17, col 29 (approximately)
- Middle: row 26, col 29 (approximately)
- Bottom: row 41, col 23 (approximately)
Wait, but the bottom yellow marker seems to be at row 41, col 23, which is not the center of the bottom ring. Let me check the segmentation data.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the level completes when the blocks form a specific pattern. Since I don't know what the target pattern is, I need to explore more.
Let me try a different approach: since (17,20) rotates all rings, and the level hasn't completed after 43 rotations, maybe the target configuration requires the rings to be in a specific alignment that I haven't reached yet.
Let me try rotating with (17,20) until the level completes. I'll batch rotate 19 more times (to reach strip=0) and check for completion.
Wait, the strip is at 19px after 43 rotations. If I rotate 19 more times, the strip would reach 0. Let me try that.
Actually, I just realized something important. The level might have a completion condition that involves the blocks forming a specific pattern when the strip reaches a specific value. For example, the level might complete when the strip reaches 0 AND the blocks are in a specific configuration.
But I don't know what the target configuration is. Let me try rotating with (17,20) until the strip reaches 0 and see if the level completes.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem. The level has 3 independent rings, and each ring can be rotated independently using different triggers. The target configuration might be one where all 3 rings are in a specific configuration.
Since (17,20) rotates all rings, and (25,13) and (34,13) rotate specific rings, I can use these triggers to align the rings independently.
But I don't know what the target configuration is. Let me try to figure it out by looking at the current image.
Looking at the current image, I notice that the blocks are arranged in a pattern. Maybe the target is a pattern where the blocks form a specific color sequence, like a rainbow or a gradient.
Actually, I just realized something. The level might have a completion condition that involves the blocks matching a specific color pattern. For example, the target might have all blocks of the same color adjacent to the yellow markers.
Let me try a different approach: I'll rotate with (17,20) until the strip reaches 0 and see if the level completes. If not, I'll need to reconsider the approach.
Let me batch rotate 19 times with (17,20) and check for completion.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# KEY FINDING:
# (17,20): rotates ALL rows [17,20,23,26,29,32,35,38,41,44]
# (25,13): rotates only row 26 (middle ring)
# (34,13): rotates only row 35 (bottom ring part)
# Strip=19px after 43 rotations

# So (17,20) is the main trigger that rotates everything.
# (25,13) and (34,13) are fine-tuning triggers for specific rings.

# Let me try rotating with (17,20) until strip reaches 0
# Strip is at 19px, so 19 more rotations needed

for i in range(19):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 17, 'col': 20}])
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed') or last_action_result.get('done'):
        print(f"Level completed after {i+1} rotations!")
        break
else:
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    green_strip = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
    if green_strip:
        b = green_strip[0]['boundary']
        strip_len = max(p[0] for p in b) - min(p[0] for p in b) + 1
        print(f"Strip after 19 more: {strip_len}px, not complete")
    
    # Let me check what the board looks like now
    # Get all colored blocks
    blocks = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ['Y', 'N', 'B']:
            b = n['boundary']
            cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//4
            cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//4
            blocks.append((cr, cc, n['color']))
    blocks.sort()
    print(f"Total colored blocks: {len(blocks)}")
    
    # Check for symmetry or pattern
    # Group by row
    from collections import defaultdict
    by_row = defaultdict(list)
    for r, c, color in blocks:
        by_row[r].append((c, color))
    
    for row in sorted(by_row.keys()):
        print(f"  Row {row}: {sorted(by_row[row])}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: (17,20) rotates ALL rings. (25,13) rotates middle ring only. (34,13) rotates bottom ring only.", "object_taxonomy": "3 rings: top(rows 17,20,23), middle(row 26), bottom(rows 29-44). 3 yellow markers.", "hud_map": "Left green strip shrinks 1px/rotation", "win_pattern": "HYPOTHESIS: strip reaches 0", "level_log": "Level 1: 8 rots. Level 2: 43 rots, strip=19, not complete.", "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 has 3 triggers with different scopes"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "(17,20)=all rings, (25,13)=middle, (34,13)=bottom. Strip=19px after 43 rots.", "goal_model": "Strip reaches 0 = completion", "recent_findings": ["(17,20) rotates all rows"], "open_questions": ["Does level complete at strip=0?"], "current_plan": "Rotate with (17,20) until strip=0", "strategy_log": ["Blind rotation didn't work", "Testing strip=0 hypothesis"], "failed_probes": ["28 rots (34,13)", "2 rots (17,20)", "11 rots (25,13)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
